sections.heading: What is Unit Testing?
sections.content: Unit testing is the practice of testing individual units of code — typically a single function, class, or module — in isolation from the rest of the system. In Android development, unit tests run on the JVM (not on a device or emulator), making them extremely fast and suitable for testing business logic, ViewModels, repositories, utility functions, and data transformations. JUnit is the standard Java/Kotlin testing framework that provides test runner infrastructure, assertions, and lifecycle annotations. Mockito is the most popular mocking library for JVM tests — it allows you to create fake implementations of dependencies so you can test a class in complete isolation. Fast, reliable unit tests are the foundation of a healthy test suite and enable confident refactoring.

rows.tests: ViewModels, Repositories, UseCases, Mappers, Utility classes

rows.tools: JUnit 4, Robolectric, Room in-memory, Hilt testing
rows.tests: Database queries, Repository with real Room, Navigation flows

rows.speed: Slow — tens of seconds per test
rows.tests: Full user flows, critical paths, regression scenarios

sections.heading: Setting Up Unit Testing Dependencies
items: JUnit 4: testImplementation("junit:junit:4.13.2") — standard test runner for Android unit tests
Mockito Kotlin: testImplementation("org.mockito.kotlin:mockito-kotlin:5.x.x") — idiomatic Kotlin Mockito wrapper
MockitoCore: testImplementation("org.mockito:mockito-core:5.x.x") — core Mockito library
Google Truth: testImplementation("com.google.truth:truth:1.x.x") — fluent assertion library, more readable than JUnit assertions
AndroidX Test Core: testImplementation("androidx.test:core-ktx:1.x.x") — for testing Android components on JVM
Coroutines Test: testImplementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-coroutines-test:1.x.x") — for testing suspend functions and flows
Turbine: testImplementation("app.cash.turbine:turbine:1.x.x") — for testing Kotlin Flow emissions
Arch Core Testing: testImplementation("androidx.arch.core:core-testing:2.x.x") — provides InstantTaskExecutorRule for LiveData testing

sections.heading: JUnit 4 Fundamentals
sections.content: JUnit 4 is the most widely used testing framework in Android development. Tests are plain Kotlin or Java classes with methods annotated with @Test. JUnit provides lifecycle annotations to run setup and teardown code before and after tests. Each test method should be independent — it should not rely on state from other tests. A good unit test follows the AAA pattern: Arrange (set up the test data and dependencies), Act (call the method under test), Assert (verify the result is correct). Test class names conventionally end with Test, and test method names should clearly describe what is being tested and what the expected outcome is.

rows.purpose: Marks a method as a test case — JUnit will discover and run it
rows.example: @Test fun `given valid input, when calculate is called, then returns correct result`() { }

rows.purpose: Runs before each test method — use for common setup like creating the class under test
Runs after each test method — use for cleanup like closing resources
Runs once before all tests in the class — must be on a companion object @JvmStatic method
Runs once after all tests in the class — must be on a companion object @JvmStatic method
Skips the test — use temporarily with a reason; avoid leaving ignored tests long-term
Applies a TestRule to modify test behavior — e.g. InstantTaskExecutorRule, MainDispatcherRule

sections.heading: Writing Your First Unit Test
items: Create test file in src/test/java/com/example/app/ mirroring the package of the class under test
Name the test class after the class being tested: HomeViewModelTest for HomeViewModel
Use backtick method names for readable test descriptions: fun `given empty list, when load is called, then state is empty`()
Follow AAA pattern: // Arrange val input = listOf(1, 2, 3); // Act val result = calculator.sum(input); // Assert assertThat(result).isEqualTo(6)
Each test should test exactly one behavior — if a test has multiple unrelated assertions it should be split
Tests must be deterministic — same input always produces same output; avoid randomness or time-dependent logic
Run tests: right-click the test class in Android Studio > Run, or ./gradlew test from terminal
View test results in Android Studio test runner panel or build/reports/tests/testDebugUnitTest/index.html

sections.heading: Mocking with Mockito
sections.content: Mocking is the practice of replacing a real dependency with a fake object that you control in tests. This allows you to test a class in isolation without needing real databases, network connections, or other external systems. Mockito lets you create mock objects, define what they return when their methods are called (stubbing), and verify that specific methods were called with expected arguments (verification). The mockito-kotlin library provides idiomatic Kotlin extensions like mock<T>(), whenever(), and argumentCaptor<T>() that work seamlessly with Kotlin's type system and null safety.

sections.heading: Mockito Core Usage
items: Create a mock: val mockRepository: UserRepository = mock() — using mockito-kotlin
Annotated mock: @Mock lateinit var mockRepository: UserRepository — requires @RunWith(MockitoJUnitRunner::class) or MockitoAnnotations.openMocks(this) in @Before
Stub a return value: whenever(mockRepository.getUser(1)).thenReturn(fakeUser)
Stub a suspend function: whenever(mockRepository.getUserAsync(1)).thenReturn(fakeUser)
Stub to throw: whenever(mockRepository.getUser(1)).thenThrow(RuntimeException("Network error"))
Verify a method was called: verify(mockRepository).getUser(1)
Verify call count: verify(mockRepository, times(2)).getUser(any())
Verify never called: verify(mockRepository, never()).deleteUser(any())
Argument matchers: any(), eq(value), anyString(), anyInt() — use when you don't care about the exact argument value
Capture arguments: val captor = argumentCaptor<User>(); verify(mockRepository).saveUser(captor.capture()); assertThat(captor.firstValue.name).isEqualTo("Alice")

rows.description: Returns the specified value when the stubbed method is called
Throws the specified exception when the stubbed method is called
Executes a lambda and returns its result — useful for dynamic responses based on input arguments
Stubs a void method to do nothing — required for Unit-returning methods
Returns different values on successive calls to the same method

sections.heading: Testing Coroutines with kotlinx-coroutines-test
items: Use runTest { } block to run suspend functions in tests — it automatically advances virtual time and handles coroutine execution

Replace Dispatchers.Main with a test dispatcher using MainDispatcherRule: class MainDispatcherRule(val dispatcher: TestDispatcher = UnconfinedTestDispatcher()) : TestWatcher() { override fun starting(desc: Description) { Dispatchers.setMain(dispatcher) }; override fun finished(desc: Description) { Dispatchers.resetMain() } }
Apply the rule: @get:Rule val mainDispatcherRule = MainDispatcherRule()
UnconfinedTestDispatcher — executes coroutines eagerly without suspending; good for simple ViewModel tests
StandardTestDispatcher — requires explicit advancement of time with advanceUntilIdle() or runCurrent(); good for precise timing tests
Advance time: advanceUntilIdle() — runs all pending coroutines to completion
Test Flow emissions with Turbine: viewModel.uiState.test { val state = awaitItem(); assertThat(state).isEqualTo(expected); cancelAndIgnoreRemainingEvents() }
Use turbine's awaitItem(), awaitComplete(), awaitError() to assert specific Flow events in sequence

rows.description: Replaces Dispatchers.Main with a test dispatcher so ViewModels that use viewModelScope can run in unit tests
Makes LiveData execute synchronously on the calling thread — required for LiveData-based ViewModel tests
Pass mock repositories and use cases to the ViewModel constructor — never test ViewModel with real network or database
Collect StateFlow values in a test coroutine using Turbine or by reading .value directly after advancing coroutines
Observe LiveData value directly via .value after triggering the action — works with InstantTaskExecutorRule
Test SharedFlow events or Channel events using Turbine — assert they are emitted exactly once

sections.heading: Fakes vs Mocks
items: Mocks — generated by Mockito; you stub specific methods and verify calls; best for simple dependencies with few methods
Fakes — hand-written in-memory implementations of an interface; best for complex dependencies like repositories

Example fake repository: class FakeUserRepository : UserRepository { private val users = mutableListOf<User>(); override suspend fun getUser(id: Int) = users.find { it.id == id }; override suspend fun saveUser(user: User) { users.add(user) } }
Fakes are more maintainable than mocks for interfaces with many methods — you implement behavior rather than stub every call
Fakes can be shared across multiple test classes — define them in the test source set
Prefer fakes for repositories and data sources; prefer mocks for analytics, loggers, and simple single-method interfaces
Fakes make tests less brittle — they don't break when you add new methods to an interface (unlike mocks that require new stubs)
Keep fakes simple — they should behave like the real implementation but use in-memory storage

rows.mistake: Testing implementation details instead of behavior
rows.fix: Test what a function does (its output and side effects), not how it does it internally — avoid verifying every internal method call

rows.mistake: Not using MainDispatcherRule for ViewModel tests
rows.fix: ViewModels use viewModelScope which requires Dispatchers.Main — without the rule, coroutines will fail with 'Module with Main dispatcher had failed to initialize'

rows.mistake: Using Thread.sleep() to wait for async operations
rows.fix: Use runTest { } with advanceUntilIdle() or Turbine — Thread.sleep() makes tests slow and flaky
Only mock dependencies of the class under test — never mock the actual class you are testing

rows.mistake: Tests depending on each other or shared mutable state
rows.fix: Create fresh instances of the class under test in @Before — each test must be fully independent

rows.mistake: Not testing error and edge cases
rows.fix: Write tests for: empty lists, null inputs, network errors, exceptions, boundary values — not just the happy path
If setup is complex, the class under test likely has too many dependencies — consider refactoring to improve separation of concerns

sections.heading: Test Naming Conventions
items: Use backtick names for descriptive test names: fun `given user is logged out, when refresh is called, then navigates to login`()
Follow the pattern: given [precondition], when [action], then [expected outcome]
Alternative pattern: [method name]_[scenario]_[expected result]: fun getUser_whenUserExists_returnsUser()
Test class name should match the class under test: HomeViewModelTest tests HomeViewModel
Group related tests using nested inner classes annotated with @Nested (JUnit 5) or use separate test files per feature
Use @DisplayName in JUnit 5 for human-readable test names in reports: @DisplayName("Given a valid user ID, when getUser is called, returns the correct user")
Avoid generic names like test1(), testSuccess(), testError() — they provide no information about what is being tested
Name test data clearly: val validUser = User(1, "Alice"); val invalidUser = User(-1, "") — makes test intent obvious

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Unit testing with JUnit and Mockito is the foundation of a robust Android test suite. Place unit tests in src/test/ for fast JVM execution, follow the AAA pattern (Arrange, Act, Assert), and use Google Truth for readable assertions. Use Mockito to mock dependencies and isolate the class under test — stub return values with whenever() and verify interactions with verify(). For coroutine-based code, use runTest { } with MainDispatcherRule and Turbine for Flow testing. Prefer fakes over mocks for complex repository interfaces. Always test edge cases and error paths alongside the happy path, keep tests independent by creating fresh instances in @Before, and use descriptive backtick method names that clearly communicate the scenario being tested. Fast, reliable unit tests enable confident refactoring and catch regressions before they reach production.
